nodes.title: Identify the inclination of a line with the x-axis
nodes.brief: Recognise the inclination as the anticlockwise angle a line makes with the positive x-axis, lying between 0 and 180 degrees.
nodes.quote: The angle (say)  $\theta$  made by the line *l* with positive direction of *x*-axis and measured anti clockwise is called the *inclination of the line*.
nodes.explanation: A line in the coordinate plane makes two supplementary angles with the x-axis. The inclination theta is the one measured anticlockwise from the positive direction of the x-axis, so it always satisfies 0 <= theta <= 180 degrees. Lines parallel to (or coinciding with) the x-axis have inclination 0 degrees, while a vertical line (parallel to or coinciding with the y-axis) has inclination 90 degrees. The inclination is the single angular quantity from which the slope of the line is later defined.
keyMoves: Measure the angle anticlockwise from the positive x-axis to the line
Restrict the inclination to the range 0 <= theta <= 180 degrees
Assign 0 degrees to a horizontal line and 90 degrees to a vertical line
misconceptions: Measuring the angle clockwise or from the y-axis instead of anticlockwise from the positive x-axis
Allowing inclinations greater than 180 degrees

nodes.title: Define the slope of a line as tan of its inclination
nodes.brief: Define slope m = tan(theta) from the inclination, and recognise when it is zero or undefined.
nodes.quote: If  $\theta$  is the inclination of a line *l*, then tan  $\theta$  is called the *slope* or *gradient* of the line *l*.
nodes.explanation: The slope (or gradient) m of a line is the tangent of its inclination: m = tan(theta), provided theta is not 90 degrees. The slope of a line whose inclination is 90 degrees is not defined, because tan(90 degrees) does not exist. As a consequence the slope of the x-axis is zero (inclination 0, tan 0 = 0) and the slope of the y-axis is not defined (inclination 90 degrees). The slope is the single number that captures the steepness and direction of a non-vertical line.
keyMoves: Compute the slope as m = tan(theta) from the inclination theta
State that the slope is undefined when theta = 90 degrees (vertical line)
Recall the slope of the x-axis is zero and the slope of the y-axis is undefined
misconceptions: Assigning a numerical slope to a vertical line instead of calling it undefined
Confusing the inclination angle itself with the slope (forgetting to take the tangent)

nodes.title: Compute the slope from the coordinates of two points
nodes.brief: Find the slope of a non-vertical line through two given points as the ratio of differences of their coordinates.
nodes.quote: Consequently, we see that in both the cases the slope m of the line through the points
nodes.explanation: A line is completely determined by two points on it, so its slope can be written in terms of their coordinates. For two points P(x1, y1) and Q(x2, y2) on a non-vertical line (so x1 is not equal to x2), the slope is m = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1). This is obtained from the right triangle formed by the horizontal and vertical separations of the two points: tan(theta) = (rise)/(run) = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1), and the same result holds whether the inclination is acute or obtuse. If x1 = x2 the line is vertical and the slope is undefined.
keyMoves: Apply m = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1) for two points on the line
Identify the rise (difference in y) over the run (difference in x)
Recognise the slope is undefined when x1 = x2 (a vertical line)
misconceptions: Writing the difference of x over the difference of y (inverting the ratio)
Being inconsistent about the order of the points in the numerator and denominator

nodes.title: Test two lines for parallelism using their slopes
nodes.brief: Use the rule that two non-vertical lines are parallel if and only if their slopes are equal.
nodes.quote: two non vertical lines  $l_1$  and  $l_2$  are parallel if and only if their slopes are equal.
nodes.explanation: If two non-vertical lines l1 and l2 are parallel, their inclinations are equal (alpha = beta), so tan(alpha) = tan(beta) and hence m1 = m2. Conversely, if their slopes are equal then by the property of the tangent function between 0 and 180 degrees their inclinations are equal, so the lines are parallel. Therefore two non-vertical lines are parallel if and only if their slopes are equal. This gives an immediate algebraic test for parallelism: just compare the two slopes.
keyMoves: Compute the slope of each line
Conclude the lines are parallel exactly when m1 = m2
Remember the test applies to non-vertical lines (both vertical lines are also parallel)
misconceptions: Thinking equal slopes only suggest, rather than guarantee, parallelism
Applying the slope test to vertical lines whose slope is undefined

nodes.title: Test two lines for perpendicularity using their slopes
nodes.brief: Use the rule that two non-vertical lines are perpendicular if and only if the product of their slopes is -1.
nodes.quote: two non-vertical lines are perpendicular to each other if and only if their slopes are negative reciprocals of each other,
nodes.explanation: If two non-vertical lines are perpendicular, their inclinations differ by 90 degrees (beta = alpha + 90 degrees), so tan(beta) = -cot(alpha) = -1/tan(alpha), giving m2 = -1/m1, i.e. m1 m2 = -1. Conversely, if m1 m2 = -1 then the inclinations differ by 90 degrees and the lines are perpendicular. Hence two non-vertical lines are perpendicular if and only if their slopes are negative reciprocals of each other, equivalently m1 m2 = -1. To test perpendicularity, multiply the two slopes and check whether the product equals -1.
keyMoves: Compute the slope of each line
Conclude the lines are perpendicular exactly when m1 m2 = -1
Equivalently, check that one slope is the negative reciprocal of the other
misconceptions: Using m1 m2 = 1 (equal product instead of -1) for perpendicularity
Taking the reciprocal without the negative sign

nodes.title: Find the acute angle between two lines from their slopes
nodes.brief: Compute the acute angle between two intersecting non-vertical lines using the tangent formula in their slopes.
nodes.quote: the acute angle (say  $\theta$ ) between lines L<sub>1</sub> and L<sub>2</sub> with slopes  $m_1$  and  $m_2$ , respectively, is given by
nodes.explanation: When two non-vertical lines L1 and L2 with slopes m1 and m2 intersect, the two adjacent angles theta and phi between them satisfy theta + phi = 180 degrees. Using theta = alpha2 - alpha1 and the tangent-subtraction formula gives tan(theta) = (m2 - m1)/(1 + m1 m2). Taking the absolute value picks out the acute angle: tan(theta) = |(m2 - m1)/(1 + m1 m2)|, valid as long as 1 + m1 m2 is not zero. The obtuse angle is then phi = 180 degrees - theta. The formula fails (denominator zero) exactly when the lines are perpendicular.
keyMoves: Apply tan(theta) = |(m2 - m1)/(1 + m1 m2)| with the two slopes
Take the absolute value to obtain the acute angle theta
Get the obtuse angle as phi = 180 degrees - theta
misconceptions: Dropping the absolute value and reporting a negative tangent or the wrong (obtuse) angle
Using the formula when 1 + m1 m2 = 0 (perpendicular lines), where it is undefined

nodes.title: Test three points for collinearity using equal slopes
nodes.brief: Decide whether three points lie on one line by checking that the slope of one segment equals the slope of an adjacent segment.
nodes.quote: Three points A, B and C are collinear, if and only if slope of AB = slope of BC.
nodes.explanation: Three points A, B and C lie on a single straight line if and only if the slope of AB equals the slope of BC. The idea is that two segments sharing the point B can only form one straight line when they have the same direction, i.e. the same slope. Computing slope of AB = (yB - yA)/(xB - xA) and slope of BC = (yC - yB)/(xC - xB) and checking equality is therefore a direct test for collinearity. (Equivalently, three points are collinear when the area of the triangle they form is zero.)
keyMoves: Compute the slope of AB and the slope of BC from the coordinates
Conclude the points are collinear exactly when these two slopes are equal
Recognise this is equivalent to the triangle ABC having zero area
misconceptions: Comparing non-adjacent segments without a shared point and mishandling the common vertex
Concluding collinearity from a near-equality without exact equality of slopes

nodes.title: Write the equations of horizontal and vertical lines
nodes.brief: Give the equation y = a or y = -a for a horizontal line and x = b or x = -b for a vertical line at a fixed distance from an axis.
nodes.quote: If a horizontal line L is at a distance *a* from the *x*-axis then ordinate of every point lying on the line is either *a* or -a
nodes.explanation: For a horizontal line at distance a from the x-axis, every point on it has the same y-coordinate (ordinate), so the equation of the line is either y = a or y = -a, the sign depending on whether the line is above or below the x-axis. Similarly, the equation of a vertical line at distance b from the y-axis is either x = b or x = -b. For example, the line through (-2, 3) parallel to the x-axis is y = 3, and the line through (-2, 3) parallel to the y-axis is x = -2. These are the simplest line equations and the base cases for the general forms.
keyMoves: Write a horizontal line as y = constant (y = a or y = -a)
Write a vertical line as x = constant (x = b or x = -b)
Choose the sign from whether the line is above/below or left/right of the axis
misconceptions: Swapping the forms: writing x = a for a horizontal line or y = b for a vertical line
Picking the wrong sign for the constant relative to the axis

nodes.title: Write a line's equation in point-slope form
nodes.brief: Derive and apply y - y0 = m(x - x0) for a line of slope m through a fixed point (x0, y0).
nodes.quote: the point (x, y) lies on the line with slope *m* through the fixed point  $(x_0, y_0)$ , if and only if, its coordinates satisfy the equation
nodes.explanation: Let P0(x0, y0) be a fixed point on a non-vertical line L of slope m, and P(x, y) any other point on L. By the definition of slope, m = (y - y0)/(x - x0), which rearranges to y - y0 = m(x - x0). Every point of L satisfies this equation and no other point in the plane does, so it is the equation of L. This point-slope form is the workhorse for writing a line's equation when one point and the slope are known; for example, the line through (-2, 3) with slope -4 is y - 3 = -4(x + 2), i.e. 4x + y + 5 = 0.
keyMoves: Identify the known point (x0, y0) and the slope m
Substitute into y - y0 = m(x - x0)
Simplify to the form Ax + By + C = 0 if required
misconceptions: Writing y - x0 = m(x - y0), mixing up coordinates and the point
Dropping the subtraction and writing y = m(x - x0) when y0 is non-zero

nodes.title: Write a line's equation in two-point form
nodes.brief: Derive and apply the equation of a line through two given points using the slope between them.
nodes.quote: Thus, equation of the line passing through the points  $(x_1, y_1)$  and  $(x_2, y_2)$  is given by
nodes.explanation: A line L through two points P1(x1, y1) and P2(x2, y2) can be found by noting that any point P(x, y) on L makes P1, P2 and P collinear, so slope of P1P equals slope of P1P2: (y - y1)/(x - x1) = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1). Rearranging gives the two-point form y - y1 = ((y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1))(x - x1). For example, the line through (1, -1) and (3, 5) is y + 1 = ((5 + 1)/(3 - 1))(x - 1), which simplifies to -3x + y + 4 = 0. This is just the point-slope form with the slope computed from the two points.
keyMoves: Compute the slope (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1) from the two points
Substitute into y - y1 = ((y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1))(x - x1)
Clear the fraction and simplify to a linear equation
misconceptions: Mismatching which point supplies (x1, y1) in the slope versus the (x - x1) factor
Inverting the slope ratio while writing the two-point form

nodes.title: Write a line's equation in slope-intercept form
nodes.brief: Use y = mx + c for a line with slope m and y-intercept c, and the variant y = m(x - d) for a given x-intercept.
nodes.quote: Thus, the point (x, y) on the line with slope *m* and *y*-intercept *c* lies on the line if and only if
nodes.explanation: If a line of slope m cuts the y-axis at a distance c from the origin (its y-intercept), it passes through (0, c), so by point-slope form y - c = m(x - 0), giving y = mx + c. The value of c is positive or negative according as the intercept is on the positive or negative side of the y-axis. A companion form uses the x-intercept d: a line of slope m with x-intercept d has equation y = m(x - d). For example, with m = 1/2 and y-intercept -3/2 the line is y = (1/2)x - 3/2, i.e. 2y - x + 3 = 0.
keyMoves: Use y = mx + c when the slope m and y-intercept c are known
Use y = m(x - d) when the slope m and x-intercept d are known
Read off slope and intercept by rearranging a given equation into y = mx + c
misconceptions: Treating c as the x-intercept rather than the y-intercept
Forgetting the sign of c when the line cuts the negative y-axis

nodes.title: Write a line's equation in intercept form
nodes.brief: Use x/a + y/b = 1 for a line with x-intercept a and y-intercept b on the axes.
nodes.quote: Thus, equation of the line making intercepts a and b on x-and y-axis, respectively, is
nodes.explanation: Suppose a line L makes an x-intercept a and a y-intercept b, meeting the axes at (a, 0) and (0, b). Applying the two-point form to these two points and simplifying gives ay = -bx + ab, which rearranges to the intercept form x/a + y/b = 1. For example, a line with intercepts -3 and 2 on the x- and y-axes is x/(-3) + y/2 = 1, i.e. 2x - 3y + 6 = 0. This form is convenient whenever the two axis intercepts are known or constrained.
keyMoves: Identify the x-intercept a and the y-intercept b
Substitute into x/a + y/b = 1
Clear denominators to get a linear equation
misconceptions: Writing x/b + y/a = 1, swapping the two intercepts
Setting the right-hand side to 0 instead of 1

nodes.title: Recognise the general equation of a line Ax + By + C = 0
nodes.brief: Recognise that every line is Ax + By + C = 0 with A, B not both zero, and convert between this and the other forms.
nodes.quote: Any equation of the form Ax + By + C = 0, where A and B are not zero simultaneously is called general linear equation or general equation of a line.
nodes.explanation: Any first-degree equation Ax + By + C = 0, where A and B are not both zero, represents a straight line and is called the general linear equation (or general equation) of a line. Every line - whether expressed in point-slope, two-point, slope-intercept or intercept form - can be rearranged into this single standard form, and conversely any such equation can be reduced back to slope-intercept form y = mx + c (slope -A/B, y-intercept -C/B when B is not zero) or to intercept form. This common form is what later distance formulas are written in terms of A, B and C.
keyMoves: Rearrange any line equation into Ax + By + C = 0
Read the slope as -A/B and y-intercept as -C/B (when B is not zero)
Reduce a general equation to slope-intercept or intercept form to find slopes and intercepts
misconceptions: Allowing both A and B to be zero (which gives no line)
Misreading the slope as A/B instead of -A/B

nodes.title: Compute the perpendicular distance of a point from a line
nodes.brief: Apply d = |A x1 + B y1 + C| / sqrt(A^2 + B^2) for the distance of a point from a line in general form.
nodes.quote: Thus, the perpendicular distance (d) of a line Ax + By + C = 0 from a point  $(x_1, y_1)$  is given by
nodes.explanation: The distance of a point from a line is the length of the perpendicular drawn from the point to the line. For a line Ax + By + C = 0 and a point (x1, y1), this perpendicular distance is d = |A x1 + B y1 + C| / sqrt(A^2 + B^2). You substitute the point's coordinates into the left side of the line equation, take the absolute value, and divide by sqrt(A^2 + B^2). For example, the distance of (3, -5) from 3x - 4y - 26 = 0 is |3(3) + (-4)(-5) - 26| / sqrt(9 + 16) = 3/5. The line must be in general form Ax + By + C = 0 before applying the formula.
keyMoves: Put the line in general form Ax + By + C = 0 and read off A, B, C
Substitute the point into A x1 + B y1 + C and take the absolute value
Divide by sqrt(A^2 + B^2) to get the distance d
misconceptions: Forgetting the absolute value, which can give a negative distance
Dividing by A^2 + B^2 instead of its square root, or applying the formula without first putting the line in general form

nodes.title: Compute the distance between two parallel lines
nodes.brief: Apply d = |C1 - C2| / sqrt(A^2 + B^2) for two parallel lines written with the same A and B.
nodes.quote: Thus, the distance d between two parallel lines  $y = mx + c_1$  and  $y = mx + c_2$  is given by
nodes.explanation: Two parallel lines have equal slopes, so they can be written y = mx + c1 and y = mx + c2. The distance between them equals the perpendicular distance from a point on one line to the other; carrying this out gives d = |c1 - c2| / sqrt(1 + m^2). When the lines are given in general form Ax + By + C1 = 0 and Ax + By + C2 = 0 (same A and B), the formula becomes d = |C1 - C2| / sqrt(A^2 + B^2). For example, the distance between 3x - 4y + 7 = 0 and 3x - 4y + 5 = 0 is |7 - 5| / sqrt(9 + 16) = 2/5. The two equations must first be written with identical A and B coefficients.
keyMoves: Write both parallel lines with the same A and B coefficients
Apply d = |C1 - C2| / sqrt(A^2 + B^2) (or |c1 - c2| / sqrt(1 + m^2) in slope form)
Take the absolute difference of the constant terms
misconceptions: Using the formula before making the coefficients of x and y identical in both equations
Adding C1 and C2 instead of taking their difference

nodes.title: Represent a straight line algebraically and solve line problems
nodes.brief: Combine slope, the various forms of a line's equation, and the distance formulas to represent any straight line algebraically and solve problems about lines.
nodes.quote: Main focus is on representing the line algebraically, for which *slope* is most essential.
nodes.explanation: The chapter studies the straight line by representing it algebraically, and slope is the central idea that makes this possible. Starting from the inclination and slope of a line, one can write its equation in whichever form suits the given data - point-slope, two-point, slope-intercept, intercept, or the general form Ax + By + C = 0 - and use slopes to test parallelism, perpendicularity, the angle between lines, and collinearity. Together with the perpendicular-distance formulas (point-to-line and between parallel lines), these tools let you set up and solve essentially any problem about straight lines: finding equations, intersections, distances, images in a line, and conditions such as concurrency.
keyMoves: Choose the equation form (point-slope, two-point, slope-intercept, intercept, or general) that matches the given data
Use slopes to handle parallelism, perpendicularity, angle, and collinearity conditions
Apply the perpendicular-distance formulas to compute point-to-line and parallel-line distances
misconceptions: Reaching for one fixed form regardless of what data is given, instead of choosing the convenient form
Treating slope, equation forms and distance as unrelated tricks rather than one connected toolkit
